summary: Today's sweep leans toward Democracy / press freedom, CS education / AI policy, Games / criticism. The strongest items in this run are ‘Being offended isn’t the worst thing. Being poor is’: how Robby Hoffman became a controversial comedy sensation, Greece scrambles to explain how explosive-packed drone landed in its waters, and Polish ex-minister flees Hungary to the US after PM Magyar says country won’t protect people wanted elsewhere.

mainItems.title: ‘Being offended isn’t the worst thing. Being poor is’: how Robby Hoffman became a controversial comedy sensation
mainItems.summary: She has suddenly become one of the world’s most successful comedians, with a hit Netflix special, an Emmy-nominated role in Hacks and another opposite Steve Carell. But many of her jokes raise hackles.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: I knew my writing students were using AI. Their confessions led to a powerful teaching moment | Micah Nathan
mainItems.summary: The problem wasn’t just the perfectly polished, yet mediocre prose. It’s what’s lost when we surrender the struggle to translate thought into words I have been teaching fiction writing at MIT since 2017.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, student agency, and school policy.

mainItems.title: The Bastl Kalimba is a wild synth that thinks it’s a thumb piano
mainItems.summary: Make no mistake, the Bastl Kalimba is a synthesizer, you just play it like a kalimba. Its tines don't really make much sound.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Dragon Quest creator thinks AI pals are "not just a convenient tool, but a friend to each individual player"
mainItems.summary: The creator of the Dragon Quest series, Yuji Horii, hopes to use advances in AI to boost the camaraderie between players and their in-game companions. Read more
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth your time if you care about design, criticism, and not just hype.

mainItems.title: Number of Whole Books Middle and High School Students Read for School Varies Widely
mainItems.summary: High school students are still reading full books in English classes — but not very many on average. And teachers working in more affluent school systems seem to assign more books than their counterparts in high-poverty communities.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, classroom practice, and educational judgment.

mainItems.title: The next Voting Rights Act must outlaw gerrymandering | Jamil Smith
mainItems.summary: The supreme court has gutted one of the strongest federal tools we had against the most effective weapon in US politics Maps can guide us home. They show us where we are, where we have been and where we might go.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Hantavirus: what happens to cruise ship passengers now and will they quarantine?
mainItems.summary: Up to 150 passengers and crew from hantavirus-hit MV Hondius start flying home aboard military and government planes from Spain’s Canary Islands The complex operation to repatriate passengers and crew of the hantavirus-hit cruise ship, the…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth watching for authoritarian drift, selective enforcement, and democratic norms.

mainItems.title: Cricut’s $99 craft cutting machine helped me feel creative again
mainItems.summary: I've always been skeptical of products that claim to help you lead a more creative life. But one recently won me over.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: A chocolate laboratory in Italy will be good for chocolate eaters — and farmers
mainItems.summary: The chocolate biz is raising the bar with a lab to assess cacao beans from around the world. (Talk about a sweet gig!) Consumers and farmers stand to benefit from the "Standard of Excellence" program.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to your current interest graph.

mainItems.title: Slay the Spire 2 beta patch cuts Act 3 boss Doormaker, introduces Bestiary
mainItems.summary: Along with numerous other tweaks and revisions, Slay the Spire 2's latest beta patch pulls Act 3 boss, Doormaker, and replaces it with brand new boss Aeonglass, as well as introducing a highly requested Bestiary. Read more
mainItems.whyItMatters: Useful if you care about how games actually work as designed experiences.

youtube.title: So, What’s the Deal with Subnautica 2? | Unpacked

new.title: Scavengers Reign
new.why: Animated speculative sci-fi with strong worldbuilding and a weirder imagination than most prestige SF.

new.title: Sugar
new.why: Noir detective structure with a genre wrinkle and a sensibility that feels adjacent to your crime/strangeness overlap.

new.title: The Lazarus Project
new.why: Time-loop espionage, moral pressure, and speculative plotting — very plausible fit.

new.title: Bodies
new.why: Time-spanning conspiracy/mystery structure that fits your taste for long-arc puzzle storytelling.

new.title: Constellation
new.why: A little more cerebral and mood-heavy, but it scratches the identity / reality-slippage itch.

new.title: The Devil’s Hour
new.why: Dark, twisty, and structurally ambitious without feeling like homework.

older.title: Counterpart
older.why: Probably the single cleanest “James show you may have missed”: espionage + parallel-world sci-fi + adult competence.

older.title: Patriot
older.why: Dry, strange, melancholy spy storytelling with a very distinct voice.

older.title: Utopia
older.why: Paranoid conspiracy energy, formal boldness, and a willingness to get strange and ugly.

older.title: Rubicon
older.why: A low-key conspiracy thriller that leans heavily on intelligence work, paranoia, and institutional atmosphere.

older.title: Continuum
older.why: A cleaner old-school sci-fi fit if you want procedural momentum wrapped around time-travel politics.

older.title: Person of Interest
older.why: Starts procedural and then quietly becomes one of the smartest AI / surveillance shows on television.

shared.title: Bad Sisters
shared.why: Darkly funny, well-observed, and ensemble-driven in a way that plausibly overlaps with Hacks / Derry Girls / Mare of Easttown energy.

shared.title: Detectorists
shared.why: Gentle, funny, humane, and specific — a softer shared-watch possibility.

shared.title: Astrid et Raphaëlle
shared.why: French procedural with a strong central duo and exactly the kind of foreign-crime appeal Maggie may share with you.

shared.title: Somebody Somewhere
shared.why: A warmer character-driven recommendation if the shared overlap leans more Bear / Good Place than pure detective work.

highlights.title: Solve a murder mystery by flying people around on your giant crane in the Disco Elysium and Chinese folktale inspired The Crane Rider's Tale
highlights.notes: Pinned from today's briefing at James's request.

highlights.title: 12 unexpected sourdough bread recipes that aren't crusty loaves: From sourdough flatbread to sourdough milk bread — and even sourdough babka.
highlights.notes: Pinned from today's briefing, the kind of bread-nerd rabbit hole worth keeping.

highlights.title: A.I. Is Prompting an Evolution, Not Extinction, for Coders
highlights.notes: Interesting piece on the current state of AI in software work.

highlights.title: The Workers Letting A.I. Do Their Jobs
highlights.notes: In an era of agents powered by artificial intelligence, many programmers are barely programming.
